section: Title, Authors, Abstract, and Introduction Part 1
text: Deep learning and alignment of spatially resolved single-cell transcriptomes with Tangram. Tommaso Biancalani, Gabriele Scalia, Lorenzo Buffoni, Raghav Avasthi, Ziqing Lu, Aman Sanger, Neiman Tokcan, Charles R. Vanderburg, Asa Segerstolpe, Meng Zhang, Inbal Avraham-Davidi, Sanja Vickovic, Mor Nitzan, Sai Ma, Ayshwarya Subramanian, Michal Lipinski, Jason Buenrostro, Nik Bear Brown, Duccio Fanelli, Xiaowei Zhuang, Evan Z. Macosko, Aviv Regev. Nature Methods, volume 18, pages 1352–1362 (2021). Abstract: Charting an organ’s biological atlas requires us to spatially resolve the entire single-cell transcriptome, and to relate such cellular features to the anatomical scale. Single-cell and single-nucleus RNA-seq (sc/snRNA-seq) can profile cells comprehensively, but lose spatial information. Spatial transcriptomics allows for spatial measurements, but at lower resolution and with limited sensitivity. Targeted in situ technologies solve both issues, but are limited in gene throughput. To overcome these limitations we present Tangram, a method that aligns sc/snRNA-seq data to various forms of spatial data collected from the same region, including MERFISH, STARmap, smFISH, Spatial Transcriptomics (Visium) and histological images. Tangram can map any type of sc/snRNA-seq data, including multimodal data such as those from SHARE-seq, which we used to reveal spatial patterns of chromatin accessibility. We demonstrate Tangram on healthy mouse brain tissue, by reconstructing a genome-wide anatomically integrated spatial map at single-cell resolution of the visual and somatomotor areas. Introduction: A Human Cell Atlas should combine high-resolution molecular and histological mapping with anatomical and functional data. Advances in single-cell and spatial genomics opened the way to high-resolution spatial profiles, but each of the currently available technologies addresses only some of the challenge of resolving entire transcriptomes in space at single-cell resolution. sc/snRNA-seq profiles single cells transcriptome-wide, but loses direct spatial information. Conversely, spatial technologies resolve transcriptomes in space, but are limited in either gene throughput or spatial resolution. Targeted in situ technologies (such as MERFISH, STARmap, smFISH) are typically limited to hundreds of preselected genes. Spatial transcriptomics methods (such as Visium, Slide-seq) spatially barcode entire transcriptomes, but with limited capture rate and a spatial resolution larger than a single cell. Computational methods have previously bridged this gap by combining single-cell and spatial measurements. However, intrinsically sparse or granularly distributed genes are difficult to predict. For measurements at coarse spatial resolution, computational deconvolution methods aim to infer cell-type composition within a spatial voxel, but are hindered by spatial 'dropouts,' where cell types defined by sparse or dim markers are not correctly detected. Here, we present Tangram, a deep-learning framework to address two challenges: learn spatial gene-expression maps transcriptome-wide at single-cell resolution, and relate those to histological and anatomical information from the same specimens. Tangram learns a spatial alignment of sc/snRNA-seq data to a reference spatial data of any kind—either fine or coarse grained.

section: Introduction Part 2, Overview, and Tangram Algorithm
text: We demonstrate by spatially mapping snRNA-seq data from the isocortex of the adult healthy mouse brain using each of five kinds of spatial supports, at different levels of resolution and gene coverage: ISH, smFISH, Visium, STARmap and MERFISH. Tangram produces consistent spatial maps of cell types and overcomes limitations in throughput or resolution. It corrects low-quality genes, even in high-resolution methods, provides single-cell resolution for low-resolution methods, and provides genome-wide coverage for targeted methods. By mapping multimodal single data (SHARE-seq) on spatial support, Tangram visualizes spatial patterns of chromatin accessibility. Finally, Tangram includes a computer vision module that leverages histological data, and maps it to anatomical positions in an existing Common Coordinate Framework (CCF) in the brain. Overview: Tangram uses sc/snRNA-seq data as 'puzzle pieces' to align in space to match 'the shape' of the spatial data. The input is sc/snRNA-seq data along with spatial profiling data from the same region, from any spatial method (e.g., MERFISH, smFISH, STARmap, ISH, or Visium), requiring only that the two modalities share at least some subset of common genes. Tangram randomly places the sc/snRNA-seq profiles in space, then computes an objective function that mimics the spatial correlation between each gene in the sc/snRNA-seq data and in the spatial data. Tangram rearranges the sc/snRNA-seq profiles in space to maximize the total spatial correlation across shared genes. The mapped sc/snRNA-seq profiles constitute the new spatial data: they now contain all genes at single-cell resolution and with spatial position. Tangram can: 1) expand from a measured subset of genes to genome-wide profiles; 2) correct low-quality spatial measurements; 3) map the location of cells of different types; 4) deconvolve low-resolution measurements to single cells; 5) resolve spatial patterns of chromatin accessibility at single-cell resolution by aligning multimodal data. Tangram Algorithm: Based on nonconvex optimization. The objective function rewards spatial alignment using two similarity functions: cell-density distributions are compared using Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, whereas gene expression is assessed through cosine similarity. If the sc/snRNA-seq data contain more cells than the spatial data, a filter term selects the optimal subset. The output is a probabilistic mapping matrix M, with dimension n_cells × n_voxels, where M_ij ≥ 0 is the probability of cell i being in voxel j. From M, we obtain a deterministic mapping by assigning the most likely cell to each spatial voxel. The objective function for mapping is defined. We introduce index i for cells, k for genes, and j for spatial voxels. S is the n_cells × n_genes expression matrix. G is the n_voxels × n_genes gene-expression matrix from spatial data. d is a n_voxel-long vector of cell densities. We learn M such that sum_j M_ij = 1. The objective function to minimize with respect to M is: Φ(M̃) = KL(m, d) - Σ_k cos_sim((M^T S)_{*,k}, G_{*,k}) - Σ_j cos_sim((M^T S)_{j,*}, G_{j,*}), where M = softmax(M̃) along voxel axis. The first term enforces learned cell density m matches expected d. The second and third terms enforce gene expression similarity. Optionally, an entropy regularizer can be added. For mapping with a filter (when n_cells > n_voxels), we introduce a filter vector f, define S^f = diag(f)·S, M^f = diag(f)·M, and m^f. The objective becomes: Φ(M̃, f̃) = KL(m^f, d) - Σ_k cos_sim((M^T S^f)_{*,k}, G_{*,k}) - Σ_j cos_sim((M^T S^f)_{j,*}, G_{j,*}) - λ_r1 Σ_{i,j} M_ij log(M_ij) + |Σ_i f_i - n_target_cells| + Σ_i (f_i - f_i^2). The last two terms are a count term and filter regularizer. Annotations transfer: Given annotation matrix A (n_cells × n_annotations, e.g., cell types), spatial annotations are A_transf = M^T A.
